retrieved_docs.title: Retinopathy of Prematurity -- Epidemiology -- Risk Factors Influencing Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several neonatal and institutional risk factors that influence the epidemiology of ROP are as follows: Gestational age and birth weight: These are the most consistent predictors. Infants born before 28 weeks or weighing <1000 g have the highest risk. Oxygen therapy practices: Inconsistent or excessive oxygen supplementation can significantly increase the incidence, especially in units without oxygen monitoring. Resource setting: In high-resource NICUs, ROP is more often restricted to extremely preterm infants, while in low-resource settings, even infants with birth weights up to 2000 g may develop ROP. NICU infrastructure and staffing: The availability of trained neonatologists, oxygen blenders, and nursing ratios influences outcomes. Screening policies: Countries with national ROP screening guidelines (eg, the United Kingdom,  the United States, Brazil) report lower rates of blindness compared to countries with fragmented or absent screening protocols. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Exchange Transfusion -- Indications -- Neonatal Hyperbilirubinemia
retrieved_docs.content: B/A mass ratio ≥8.0 (molar ratio 0.9) for newborns with gestational age 38 weeks or older and no neurotoxicity risk factors B/A mass ratio ≥7.2 (molar ratio 0.81) for newborns with gestational age 38 weeks or older and at least 1 neurotoxicity risk factor B/A mass ratio ≥7.2 (molar ratio 0.81) for newborns with gestational age 35 through 37 weeks and no neurotoxicity risk factors B/A mass ratio ≥6.8 (molar ratio 0.77) for newborns with gestational age 35 through 37 weeks and at least 1 neurotoxicity risk factor

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing Enterocolitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing enterocolitis is a disease affecting neonates and is the most common life-threatening emergency affecting the gastrointestinal tract of infants in the neonatal intensive care unit. Necrotizing enterocolitis typically occurs in the second to third week of life. Several risk factors have been identified, but prematurity, low birth weight, and formula feeding have been identified as primary risks [2] . Specifically, high osmotic strength formula feeding has been implicated as a risk factor. Genetic factors may also play a role [1] . Incidence worldwide varies between 0.3 to 2.4 infants per 1000 live births. Nearly 70% of these cases occur in premature infants born before 36 weeks gestation. Necrotizing enterocolitis affects 2% to 5% of all premature infants and is responsible for nearly 8% of all NICU admissions. Overall, mortality ranges from 10% to 50%. However, in the most severe cases, involving perforation, peritonitis, and sepsis, mortality approaches 100%.

retrieved_docs.title: Infantile Colic (Nursing) -- Risk Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Infantile colic is estimated to affect 3% to 28% of infants worldwide. [9] The reported prevalence of infantile colic varies across studies; some say higher than 20%. Symptoms peak around six weeks of age and decline gradually after that. However, the recognition of colic depends on parental perception of crying intensity and duration, which is subjective and difficult to quantify. Parental well-being and cultural differences in acceptable crying may also affect these data. Regardless of the variability in prevalence, there is no proven association with gender, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, feeding preference, or birth order.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever in a Neonate -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the laboratory data, you can risk stratifying the well-looking febrile infants greater than 21 days of age with no risk factors and with no source of invasive infection into high risk, medium risk, and low risk.  You can safely observe low-risk infants at home or in the hospital without empiric treatment, meanwhile, high-risk infants are observed and treated in the hospital. You also can observe medium-risk infants in the hospital or at home before treating them empirically. [11] If in doubt admit them to the pediatric unit for observation while waiting for the culture results. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypoglycemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Early initiation of breastfeeding is crucial for all infants. For asymptomatic infants at risk of neonatal hypoglycemia, the AAP recommends initiating feeds within the first hour of life and performing initial glucose screening 30 minutes after the first feed. [3] The AAP recommends goal blood glucose levels equal to or greater than 45 mg/dL prior to routine feedings, and intervention for blood glucose <40 mg/dL in the first 4 hours of life and <45 mg/dL at 4 to 24 hours of life. [1] [3] The best intervention for asymptomatic hypoglycemia is to increase feeding frequency. [5] Increasing breastfeeding is challenging for some infants. Difficulties with infant latching, poor feeding, and low volumes of breast-milk may interfere with the successful establishment of early breastfeeding in the first hours of life. Most commonly, infant formula is the choice for asymptomatic neonatal hypoglycemia in late preterm and term infants. [5] Formula is inexpensive, readily available, easy to give, and has a high carbohydrate content, resulting in a rapid rise of blood glucose concentrations within minutes. [12] However, the use of infant formula risks interrupting establishment of breastfeeding and alters the neonatal microbiome, potentially increasing the risk of infections and allergies. [5] Blood glucose levels should be re-checked one hour after feeding. It is uncertain whether early episodes of low glucose in the first 48 hours of life need correction in asymptomatic, healthy infants without risk factors for hypoglycemia. [5] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: High-Frequency Oscillator in the Neonate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The major risk factors for the development of CLD include immature lungs, oxygen toxicity, and ventilator-induced lung trauma. [2] [3] The term bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) was introduced in 1967 by Northway et al, [4] and is still used to describe infants who are dependent on oxygen beyond 28 days after birth. [5] While the pathogenesis of BPD has many factors, invasive mechanical ventilation is a primary risk factor. The most common cause of morbidity of prematurity is BPD. For this reason, research has focused on lung protective strategies to limit the development of CLD.

retrieved_docs.title: Hearing Loss Assessment in Children -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: All newborns should be screened for hearing loss before being discharged from the hospital. Those who pass the initial screening should follow up in their primary care medical home. Those who do not pass should be re-tested before discharge, with repeat testing occurring at least several hours after the initial screening. [7] In the well-baby nursery, this testing is often performed using OAEs. Newborns with risk factors for hearing loss should receive screening with AABR testing due to the higher risk of ANSD in these patients. Significant risk factors for hearing loss in children include care in the neonatal intensive care unit (NICU) for 5 days or longer, hyperbilirubinemia requiring exchange transfusion, culture-proven sepsis, and administration of ototoxic medications. [3] Newborns cared for in the NICU have been shown to have a 6.9 times higher risk of hearing loss than newborns who do not require NICU care. Additional risk factors include in-utero infection, neonatal infection, and craniofacial abnormalities or syndromic conditions. [11] The risk of ANSD in healthy newborns is low enough that ABR is not a requirement for those who pass initial OAE screening.

retrieved_docs.title: Small for Gestational Age (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: As per the Neonatal Resuscitation Program, in preparation for delivery, a dedicated, qualified individual should attend every birth, whose only responsibility is the assessment and care of the newborn. In the case of a delivery with suspected risk factors such as FGR, 2 qualified individuals should be present with a full team equipped for extensive resuscitation, including intubation and CPR, immediately available if called. Care of the FGR SGA infant after birth should begin as it does for any infant, with a quick assessment of the infant’s gestational age, tone, and breathing. If the infant appears to have a good tone and adequate breathing, the initial steps of warming and drying, positioning the airway, and clearing any secretions can be done on the mother’s chest or abdomen; otherwise, the infant should be moved to the warmer for more extensive assessment and resuscitation. Following stabilization, newborn care for the term FGR SGA infant can typically be done in the mother’s room or newborn nursery per institutional policy. However, preterm infants need admission and continuing care for issues related to their prematurity in the neonatal intensive care unit. [40]

retrieved_docs.title: High-Frequency Oscillator in the Neonate -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Greater than 75% of infants born less than 27 weeks gestation require some form of mechanical ventilation to survive. [1] While survival rates for extremely premature (less than 28 weeks) or very low-birth-weight (VLBW) infants have increased, the incidence of chronic lung disease (CLD) from ventilator-induced lung injury remains high. [2] For this reason, effective lung ventilation and oxygenation are key in preventing CLD.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Hypertension -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: No standardized guidelines currently outline the optimal timing for initiating antihypertensive therapy or managing mild cases in neonates. Since many affected infants require only short-term pharmacologic intervention and improve spontaneously over time, careful clinical judgment must guide treatment. Decisions should be individualized, based on the infant’s overall health status, the presence of end-organ involvement, and contributing risk factors. This cautious, case-by-case approach helps balance the benefits of treatment with the potential risks associated with unnecessary medication exposure in this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: Periventricular and Intraventricular Hemorrhage -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The care of the preterm infant requires highly specialized facilities that employ an interprofessional team including a pediatrician, neonatologist, intensivist, radiologist, neurosurgeon, obstetrician, and neonatal nurses). Born outside a perinatal tertiary center is a risk factor for PIVH. [10] When caring for the infant, there has been a demonstrated benefit that reduces the risk of PIVH in the preterm infant when delivery is in a facility that specializes in the care of very low birth weight children (13.2% vs. 27.4%). [1] The implementation of a care bundle for the management of premature infants reduced the incidence of PIVH from 34.8% to 26.3% in all grades, but the reduction is most noted in its most severe forms (grades III and IV). [10]

retrieved_docs.title: High-Frequency Oscillator in the Neonate -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: In 2015, Cools et al. reviewed 19 RCTs that compared HFOV to conventional ventilation in preterm and VLBW infants and found no differences in IVH between the groups. [11] While the group identified a decreased risk of BPD in using the HFOV, this effect was not seen in all studies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 0 -- Community 12
retrieved_docs.content: Query “disorders of the newborn” (SMQ) includes extreme prematurity (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), intracranial hemorrhage, and unspecified perinatal infection, reinforcing the interconnectedness of extreme prematurity, fetal growth restriction, perinatal infection, hemorrhagic and hemolytic disorders, and birth‑related trauma. These hierarchical classifications support diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic interventions throughout the perinatal and early infant periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.content: these entities form an interconnected network that integrates obstetric, perinatal, neonatal screening, and renal‑surgical conditions, supporting comprehensive risk stratification, coordinated maternal‑fetal care, and ongoing management of delayed postpartum effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: Certain conditions originating in the perinatal period (P00‑P96) are situated within the broader category of Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation, which is classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}. Within the perinatal‑originating conditions, there is a more specific subgroup of Respiratory and cardiovascular disorders specific to the perinatal period (P19‑P29). This hierarchical organization indicates that infants born with extreme prematurity and unspecified low birth weight are at risk for a range of perinatal conditions, including distinct respiratory and cardiovascular disorders, highlighting the importance of recognizing the underlying gestational and growth‑related factors when assessing and managing newborn health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), and short stature (قصر القامة) is clinically associated with a threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر). This interrelationship indicates that infants born preterm with low birth weight are more likely to develop reduced height, and that reduced height itself may signal an increased risk of threatened premature labor. Recognizing these connections can help clinicians monitor growth patterns and anticipate obstetric complications, thereby improving patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: in the context of hemolytic and perinatal jaundice, providing quantitative guidance for diagnosis. The disorder “Disorder relating to short gestation AND/OR low birthweight” is both a child of liveborn and an occurrence within the Neonatal qualifier, emphasizing the heightened risk of neonatal‑specific complications in preterm or low‑birth‑weight infants. In the SMQ “اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة”, three entities are explicitly enumerated: “ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}” (birth at the highest gestational age with unspecified weight), “نزيف مخي وليدي” (intracranial and possibly spinal hemorrhage in the newborn), and “عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك” (unspecified perinatal infection). Recognizing these entities as members of the SMQ enables systematic identification, coding, and reporting of important newborn disorders. Collectively, this hierarchical framework integrates liveborn status, gestational duration, fetal growth restriction, reproductive
